result:
I want to write about the peculiar intimacy of laundromats at midnight.

There's something about fluorescent lights humming over rows of tumbling clothes that strips people bare in ways conversation never could. You learn everything watching someone fold their laundry. The careful way a man smooths his work shirts—mechanical, practiced, done a thousand times. The woman who separates everything into impossible precision, even the socks. Someone else who just shoves it all into one sagging bag, defeated.

I used to live above a laundromat in Queens. Thursday nights, 11 PM, I'd take my basket down with a book I never read. The machines created this industrial white noise, rhythmic and mindless, and people would talk in ways they wouldn't at noon. A nurse told me about watching her first patient die. An older man explained how to fix a carburetor, though I'd never asked, though I don't drive. We weren't friends. We never exchanged names. But there was something in the shared vulnerability of public cleanliness, this admission that we all wear dirty clothes, that we all need to start over every few days.

The dryers have windows. I used to watch my clothes fall and lift, fall and lift, like something being born or dying. Cotton, denim, the occasional sock escaping to the drum's edge, clinging. Everything warm afterward. Everything temporarily clean.

I have a washer now, in my apartment. It's convenient. I don't miss the laundromat, exactly. But I miss that strange democracy, that unearned closeness, the way strangers become briefly visible to each other under too-bright lights, waiting for the spin cycle to end.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
